Financials Down on Fed Doubts -- Financials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of banks and other financial institutions fell, amid growing uncertainty about the Federal Reserve's next steps.

Statements from Fed officials and odds on futures markets suggest it's now a tossup between a rate cut and a pause in December, The Wall Street Journal reported.

Insurance giant American International Group said its incoming president, John Neal, the former head of British bank Lloyds will no longer be joining the insurance giant "due to personal circumstances."

JPMorgan Chase is seeking to have a court order to pay Charlie Javice's $74 million legal fees in a fraud case the bank mounted against the startup founder overturned, alleging further incidences of fraud in the expense claims.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 1755ET

Consumer Cos Slip Amid Holiday Shopping Concerns -- Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies slipped, compounding losses from earlier in the week, amid concerns about the key holiday shopping season.

Economists say the consumer sector has seen a bifurcation, with luxury brands catering to wealthy consumers thriving even as middle-class providers, such as Target, struggle.

Swiss luxury firm Richemont, the owner of upscale brands including jeweler Cartier, reported a pickup in demand with fiscal second-quarter sales rising a robust 14%.

In an acknowledgment of middle-class struggles making ends meet, the Trump administration said there were plans to roll back tariffs on key grocery items.

Walmart Chief Executive Doug McMillon stepped down from his position after more than 15 years with the company, to be succeeded by President John Furner.

Shares of driving range chain Topgolf Callaway rose after reports that private-equity firm Leonard Green was in talks to buy the chain out for about $1 billion.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Health Care Down, But Not by Much, as Deal Activity Continues -- Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies fell, but not by as much as the broad market, as deal activity in the sector continued.

Merck struck a $9.2 billion deal to buy biotechnology company Cidara Therapeutics in a move that bolsters the drugmaker's respiratory portfolio and pipeline as it braces for the looming loss of patent exclusivity for the blockbuster cancer drug Keytruda.

Closely held Purdue Pharma won bankruptcy court approval for an opioid-related settlement of at least $7.4 billion, clearing a path for the OxyContin maker to exit its six-year bankruptcy and resolve mass lawsuits by governments, health-care providers and individuals.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 1741ET

Industrials Flat on Rate Fears -- Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies were more or less flat, and retreated modestly for the week, amid fears about the interest-rate and economic outlook.

The European Union's competition regulator conditionally cleared Abu Dhabi's state oil company's $13.7 billion bid for German plastics maker Covestro after an in-depth foreign subsidies probe.

data.title: PayPal Establishes Commercial Paper Program to Access Up To $5 Billion
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


PayPal is establishing a commercial paper program to access up to $5 billion in debt for general corporate purposes.

The fintech company said Friday that it would issue commercial paper notes of varying maturities, up to 397 days from the date of issuance. It said it would use its existing lines of credit to serve as a liquidity backstop for the program.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 1735ET

data.title: Soros Fund Management dissolves share stake in Coreweave
Materials Down as Dollar Gains -- Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals and other raw materials fell as the U.S. dollar gained in light of mounting doubts about the Federal Reserve's December policy statement.

The Fed Funds futures markets now indicate that the decision will be a toss-up between another rate cut and a pause, with increasingly vocal proponents of each action. Strategists at one brokerage said the Fed's minutes will likely provide further evidence of this split.

On Thursday, a Trump administration official told reporters that the White House plans to eliminate tariffs on coffee, beef, bananas and some textiles and apparel from four Latin American countries: Argentina, Ecuador, El Salvador and Guatemala.

Brazilian meatpacker JBS, the largest beef processor in the U.S., is leaning into the pricier Wagyu and expanding ground-beef production to help cope with rising cattle costs, as the U.S. cattle herd shrinks to its smallest size since the 1950s.

Gold futures and silver futures fell significantly for the session due to interest-rate concerns, but remained higher for the week following a record close for silver Wednesday.

BHP Group intends to appeal a decision by the English High Court that said it is liable for the deadly collapse of the Fundao dam in Brazil 10 years ago.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

11-14-25 1733ET

Energy Up on Rotation Back Into Sector -- Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies rose sharply as traders rotated back into the sector.

Oil futures recovered the $60-per-barrel level following Ukrainian drone attacks on a Russian Black Sea port.

Pipeline operator Enbridge will push ahead with a $1.4 billion expansion of its core network to boost deliveries of Canadian heavy oil and reach key refining markets in the U.S. Midwest and Gulf Coast.

Iran seized a Cyprus-registered fuel tanker, shipping companies said, its first such interdiction in the Strait of Hormuz in more than a year.

data.description:

Berkshire reveals new $4.3 billion Alphabet stake, sells more Apple
data.description:
(Reuters) -Berkshire Hathaway revealed a $4.3 billion stake in Google parent Alphabet and further reduced its stake in Apple, detailing its equity portfolio for the last time before Warren Buffett ends his 60-year run as chief executive officer.

In a filing on Friday with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, Berkshire said it owned 17.85 million Alphabet shares as of September 30.

Berkshire lowered its Apple stake to 238.2 million shares from 280 million in the third quarter, and has now sold nearly three-quarters of the 905 million shares it once held. Apple remained Berkshire's largest stock holding, at $60.7 billion.

The filing listed Berkshire's U.S.-listed stock holdings as of September 30, which comprised most of the conglomerate's $283.2 billion equity portfolio.

Berkshire's investment in Alphabet is surprising given Buffett's usual value-investing style and aversion to investing in technology companies.

Buffett considers Apple, which makes the iPhone, more of a consumer products company.

It is not clear whether Buffett, his portfolio managers Todd Combs and Ted Weschler, or CEO-designate Greg Abel make specific purchases, though Buffett normally makes larger investments.

At Berkshire's annual shareholder meeting in 2019, Buffett and late Vice Chairman Charlie Munger lamented not investing in Google. Buffett said its advertising model bore similarities to what was working for Berkshire's Geico car insurance unit.

"We screwed up," Munger said.

"He's saying we blew it," Buffett responded.

Alphabet shares rose 1.7% in after-hours trading.

(Reporting by Jonathan Stempel in New York; Editing by Matthew Lewis)

By Jonathan Stempel
